Had he have turned to Superman and gone "pretty clever, huh? Huh? Do you get it? One thousand monkeys on one thousand type writers?" Then no, it wouldn't have been Lex. As it is, Holt nails the petulant and vindictive billionaire perfectly.

replies.author: @galaxycraftings
replies.text: @dosomestuff1949ooh so what ur looking for is ‘superhero but in our world’ but I think we’ve reached the limit of that at this point. When you do that, u greatly limit what movies can adapt from comic books, you also limit the viewers imagination. The original fantastic four had to have a ‘gas galactus’ in order to avoid appearing ‘goofy’ when adapting him. Fan4stic was ‘realistic’ military outfit dogwater. Practically every live action Batman outside of affleck can’t fight half of his rogues gallery because directors go for ‘realistic’ instead of actually adapting the comic book. Creating a unique universe for these adaptions is long overdue, also Superman had go writing, character development, all of that

replies.author: @dosomestuff1949
replies.text: @galaxycraftings Superman was NOT a well written film. It was all over the place. Many arcs and plot lines either get dropped or don't get resolved properly. Superman had a bit of a character arc, but it was done as an after thought rather than being the focal point of the film. Overall I think the movie was fun and entertaining, and gave us a great Superman, but it didn't do much beyond that. What I was hoping for was a world that starts of like our world, and SLOWLY becomes more like a comic book world, that way it actually feels EARNED.

replies.author: @Tyler-kr8vp
replies.text: I don't really understand most of the criticism. Having an army of monkeys to farm engagement is probably the most contrived part of the narrative, but who cares. Lex could've executed superman whenever he wanted but the point of the story is for him to bolster his relationship with the government and get permission to do so. It's not the best explanation but this is a superhero movie, you don't need to hyper analysis everything. He also acts as if Lex being able to create a pocket dimension invalidates his desire to rule over earth, which is stupid. replies.author: @aquilessd2536
replies.text:
​@jacobstevens7046you said it yourself THE FILM!!!!

The character is the same, but people is weird and driven by selfishness, self-preservation instinct and other traits, making them lack the awareness that a character (or a person) in a movie has a lot of layers and intentions (a theme we saw in the movie).

Superman is not a simple character, is the opposite, that is why Batman is basic, people can understand the character in 2 seconds: "the murder of his parents driven him to protect people and deliver justice in order they don't suffer what he experienced". That is why Superman is so complex, it is not easy to explain he has a lot of layers, so in reality more people don't known and understand Superman.

That is why this movie is absurdly obvious in showing to the "not so aware fans" how Clark Kent Superman acts and who he is

Characters are the same, the Clark in Man of Steel and Superman (2025) both are the same character, they are willing to save people, they care and protect. The difference is that this movie shove it literally into the fans eyes with a lot of reminders in every scenes in order to get the message delivered by exposing it to the people that doesn't get that about to Superman character.

Which is Strange because, the character is almost 100 years old and still people seem to miss the point, which is also included in a scene of the movie. (The final scene when Clark talks to Luthor) 
Returning to Batman; everyone understands the character, even small children can understand him, while his motivations appeared as complex in reality are simple, he is driven by his parents dead, to become someone that protects people in order other people don't suffer the same, so people identify (a lot) with the character, but Superman in the other hand, appears as a simple character, but his emotional deepness and profoundness is monumental, people can not understand how a person like Clark with all his abilities and powers can be good, but that is also taken into the movie with the Lois and Clark scene at Clark's appartment. Lois told to Clark he is willing to see good in everyone, but she can't.

The choice to do a better world.

replies.author: @lt.pickle6208
replies.text:
@aquilessd2536I disagree. This comes back to my argument of how badly he’s been represented up till now. Recent iterations of Superman paint him to be this complex, morally grey character that is conflicted with saving humanity despite not being human himself. 
That he feels distant towards them bc of his kryptonian heritage. It makes for a compelling story on paper, but misses the entire point of Clark Kent. 

Clark Kent! That’s what MoS and BvS get wrong. Clark is a very simple character; he’s a farm boy from Kansas that was imbued with the best of humanity from Martha & John Kent and decides to use his alien gifts to help & inspire humanity. His countryside upbringing allows him to enjoy the simple things in life & to appreciate life & love even more. Yes he sees the worst side of us from time to time (Lex) but still chooses good. Which Superman (2025) gets right :) 

Also I agree with your Batman take, everyone knows who he is & his motivations are understandable. But considering the decade long misrepresentation of Superman I feel James Gunn HAD to shove the ideals of Superman in our faces, to remind the general public what this character stands for. A bit of a nitpick for people I can understand, but given the context it doesn’t bother me much.

If there’s one criticism I can say about this movie is the Supes is locked up for the majority of the film which kinda blows but oh well

replies.text:
That's fine but I hope you recognise that as soon as the literal first 5 minutes of the film Lex already nearly killed Superman. Ultraman beat him so badly he physically didn't have the energy to get back the fortress of solitude. If Krypto or the robots didn't exist there's a significant chance Superman could have just died in the arctic.

replies.author: @themindeclectic9821
replies.text: Lex Luthor and Superman are one of the greatest villain/hero combos in the world because they are very much a worldview conflict, not a personal one. Superman is altruism and goodness because it's the right thing to do, radically kind, and Lex Luthor is the cynic who cannot believe someone is truly good for goodness's sake and when they can't prove otherwise they can't live with themselves in a world where genuine goodness remains so they have to destroy it or die trying. It's almost like Javert killing himself because he can't just accept that people can change or that a thief can be good. Valjean had his life in his hand and in goodness gave it back to Javert, and Javert can't live in debt to a thief and can't reconcile a thief being anything other than Bad. "It is either Valjean or Javert" and it is either Superman or Lex Luthor. Either altruism exists, or it doesn't. And it's easier to say it doesn't and not have to hold yourself accountable for your self centered actions than it is to accept the proof that good people exist and you now have an innate moral duty to be one. Superman is that proof.

replies.author: @theamazinggarbage3209
replies.text:
⁠​⁠​⁠​⁠​⁠​⁠​⁠​⁠​⁠​⁠​⁠​⁠​⁠​⁠​⁠​⁠​⁠​⁠@fred6907Somewhat agree with your point that there are a lot of characters, outside of Mr terrific the other two members of the justice team were kinda sidelined until the end of the movie. With that being said I don’t think it egregious enough that it actually harms the movie in any real way. The plot stayed on course and most of the supporting characters were explored enough that I had a basic idea on what role each of them would play in the story moving forward by on from here.

I know humor is subjective but it really isn’t as bad as you’re arguing it is. The movie separates the funny moments from the serious ones pretty well, so it’s not like you’re being bombarded with “bad” humor anyway. The humor felt real, rather than artificial and that’s all that really matters in the end. #supershit and the harem bit got a chuckle out me so it wasn’t completely dry.

Note btw that this is coming from someone who watched angryjoes review before seeing the film. I came in expecting it to be subpar, and was subsequently surprised when I had a pretty good time. The vibes gave of “classic Superman” and the movie had a lot of good moments, like the breakout scene and his final fight against luthertech.

Just another reminder that we should probably stop seeing movie reviewer words as law. Wasn’t going to watch it until my dad suggested we watch in on max, and I came out pretty satisfied with my use of time.

I enjoyed it... there were good moments.  But overall it was bad because it felt so shallow... like a GenZ mockery of the world.  I guess in that regard it's accurate, GenZ doesn't take life seriously, everything is a joke or a game to them.

Try this, GenZ, lose all your family, lose your phone, loser internet, lose your friends... all of them.  Lose everything in the world that matters to you... and now try to survive in that world where you're cut off from everything that socially connected you to the world.  Try survive without connection... assume you don't die or implode... there's nobody to complain to... there's only you surviving in social isolated pain.... that's the movie that is interesting.

replies.author: @ronny2shoes
replies.text: The movie was terrible. I just watched it myself and I was not impressed whatsoever. The tone is completely unserious, even in moments when it should be serious. The casting is awkward and there are clear liberal themes being pushed (Lois being a super badass and having to 'do it herself!' because her male coworkers can't; she is able to fly the black guy's aircraft which makes zero sense). They gave Clark's parents an overly ridiculous southern accent and clueless ditzy attitude that comes off as forced. There is zero chemistry between Clark and his parents in this movie. Gunn is a weirdo and should be nowhere near anymore major movies in the future. I have seen enough.

There was not a drop of the MESSAGE in this movie. Lois fell off the page of comic books and the animated series straight into this movie, and with the exception of the dick with a mustache her coworkers were decent and competent. His parents were folksy. I don’t know how much time you’ve spent in the Midwest but I have spent plenty and I was unoffended by the portrayal. Zero chemistry? Whatever

replies.author: @nickhayden7657
replies.text:
Whilst he makes some good points, I don’t agree with Drinker’s over all appraisal of Superman tbh, me and my partner had a blast. We were both entertained and that’s kinda the point no? 

I feel like it’s a waste of time for Drinker to interact with comments/people that don’t agree with him on the basis that he’s already openly stated his opinion in the video. I guess it’s more an invitation for his audience to disagree with him and start a conversation amongst themselves. 

Also. I don’t really feel like going after his content is relevant. I haven’t seen Rogue Elements or read any of his books, but it must take serious guts for a critic of his notoriety to attempt to make his own content - surely he knows he’s going to be targeted and put through the ringer. 

I absolutely don’t agree with some of Drinker’s world views, but do I think his voice is important and it’s driving a conversation which is ultimately holding creative studios to account; forcing them to focus on creating decent content, rather than pushing their political agendas - which can only be a good thing for consumers surely?

author: @StaticSignaI
text:
Just saw Superman and honestly… it kinda hit the spot. It’s not perfect, but it feels like Superman again. There’s heart, there’s hope, and thank God it isn’t drowning in darkness or pretending to be something it’s not. The new guy (Corenswet) did well, and Rachel Brosnahan is a badass Lois. Their chemistry carries a lot.

Some scenes are a little overstuffed and the tone jumps around at times, but when it works, it works. Krypto is a damn scene stealer too. This definitely isn’t Snyder’s Superman it’s more classic comic book energy with a modern spin. If you’re into that, I think you’ll walk out smiling.

Yeah, well, I will re-watch C. Reeve's SM for free rather than watch this overrated slop for money at the cinema. Reeve's SM is heroic, stoic and strong, his film has plenty of heart and hope.
Way too many people let their viewing standards so low in the recent years, they are hitting the ground.
For the amount of money and other resources available to Gunn and the major studios, there is zero excuse for this.

replies.author: @ruddo1970
replies.text:
@OldManRising I certainly understand that decision as i've made it myself a bit lately with the Marvel offerings. 
For me this is a story harkening back to the Christopher Reeve era, with a more hopeful, optimistic Superman. It certainly packs too much in in too short a time. Like the 78 movie it probably needed a longer run time in the end.
Like Christopher Reeve, this Superman looks strongly built without being overly muscled. Cavill was a level above with his physical presence yes. Either works for me, tho Corenswet certainly towers over everyone in many shots where Cavill didn't imo.
The many criticisms brought up in all the reviews i've seen are valid of course but the positives are there as well. I'll be surprised if you find any 'woke' or overtly political messaging in it. Only Gunns slightly feminine depiction of the masculine man perhaps. I somewhat understand reviews talking about it without noticing it myself.

I'm glad you enjoyed it, but there are still a large number of people that didn't, or thought it was meh. 

I thought it was mid at best, but only because of pros and cons, which were inconsistent. What it did well, typically knocked it out of the park, but what it didn't do well was completely avoidable and quite a letdown. 

I am looking forward to a second instalment, though. I'd like to see this version of the characters continue onwards before potentially teaming up with other heroes.

replies.author: @Ninety6DeShae
replies.text:
@Gaiden-Shinji875 yea it’s not to say I don’t understand why some people didn’t like it. It’s not a perfect movie by any means. 

It’s hard to make a good Superman movie because the character isn’t relatable to anyone. I think this might be the most relatable live action version of Superman. 

Plus I enjoyed the comic book feeling it gave, and I love how we just we got thrusted into a fully developed universe already. 

It’s gonna be interesting to see what DC does from here, and I really hope they don’t fumble it.

replies.author: @Gaiden-Shinji875
replies.text:
​@tendo_no_entiendo I am actually glad people liked it and was very surprised at the lack of any politicking because it will hopefully encourage/inspire others to adopt that approach.

I regard the casting as one of the strengths (despite being bitter about Henry Cavill having to leave). To be fair to Corenswet he does make a good Superman, and Rachel is a fantastic Lois. 

I'd give it a 6 at most, but I agree with what you said about the DC movie standards. By that metric it's one of their best movies in a very long time.

replies.text: ⁠@ronny2shoes lmao I WISH I was paid to rate movies. I completely disagree with you, man. The characters’ chemistry was a 10. I can understand the pacing being too much for people, but I knew going in that when the camera cut to black, title cards were originally in place telling you the next day has started. But ultimately I like how Gunn didn’t assume we were all dumb and could figure that out on our own. I’ve watched the movie 3 times already and I haven’t done that since The Batman.. The movie is great, and I love that so many people love it as much as me. I’m 29, and this is the best live action version of Superman in my lifetime. Wasn’t a huge James Gunn fan before this, but he’s made me one with how great he portrayed Superman in this film.

author: @ZakEmber
text: "Illegal Immigrant" - this is just flat-out wrong, and it's right to challenge this. If anyone *_reads Superman #53_* where they first went into depth over his arrival to Earth, it's clear that Kal-El was an *abandoned child.* Legal rights to his guardianship transferred to the state of Kansas on his arrival, and the state of Kansas allowed Jon and Martha Kent to adopt him, which included the process of having full U.S. Citizenship. Right there in the panels Jon and Martha reported Kal to the state and told the truth: they had found an abandoned child and asked to adopt him, and the official even said, "we investigate applicants very carefully, you know." (Jon and Martha passed the selection process, which can take years.)  Clark has a birth certificate, social security number, and has to pay taxes. An illegal immigrant (in the vast majority of cases) makes the choice to move into a country illegally for the purposes of settling; Kal-El is best described as an abandoned infant refugee who was legally adopted and granted citizenship. It's only modern writers and their ignorance that try to re-write this in order to try to repaint a classic hero in the lens of their politics regarding illegal immigration, conflating Clark's stateless abandoned child scenario with a full adult knowingly crosses into a country undeclared for the purpose of settling, and does this ignoring the new country's laws.

text: I saw the movie and funny enough, everything the Drinker says about this film, I DISAGREE with. This movie was beautiful and while it wasn’t perfect, the flaws were completely drowned out by the positive aspects of the film. I’d watch it again and I don’t say that about many films. I usually agree with the drinker, but this review felt like he was reaching for something negative to say to fill time. Oh well, I can’t wait to watch it again.

replies.author: @louisalex98
replies.text:
I entered expecting a James Gunn movie and besides some camera shots, he respected the characters the tone and the story. I've never seen Superman so... hopeful. That's literally the big blue on the screen.
Yes, Lex is peckish because that who he is, eternally jealous by always dangerous, the monkeys on a typewriter the pocket dimension, it's accurate to Lex vecause he made his MISSION to defeat Superman in every single way.
I understand why it's not an origin story per-se, but it *is* and origin story, it drastically changes the characters and a perfect jumping point for the DCU.
All the side characters in this movie are not to be understood every way, it's supposed to compel us to understand them in their own movies.
I'm dissapointed with the Critical Drinker this time... He sounds like he went to see with his mind already up, everyone has bias, but that sounded a bit like "i don't like comic book elements in my comic book movie".

replies.author: @margarethmichelina5146
replies.text:
I'm not gonna saying this movie is perfect either but I agree. Superman is a symbol of hope and justice and I knew is gonna be chessy and colorful. Just like original Superman movie starring Christopher Reeve, that was also cheesy and didn't take it seriously like how Superman going back in time just to revive Lois from the dead. 
But, I just don't like how the jokes are Marvel-esque most of the time, like come on, James, this ain't GOTG and how he makes the twist that Supes's Kryptonian parents want him to impregnate women in Earth to keep the Kryptonian civilization alive. Hold up, isn't that Ego's backstory from GOTG 2 when he's also wanting Peter Quill doing the same thing? And isn't that Omni-Man's backstory? What the fuck? 
I'm more understand of Thomas Wayne being a shady billionaire (let's be honest, most of them are) more than how Supes' parents are evil.

replies.author: @LukeKendall-author
replies.text:
The monkeys thing was an allusion to "infinite monkeys producing Shakespeare" while poking fun at internet trolls.
The pocket universe was tech that's so stupidly dangerous it shouldn't have been used, but showed how Luthor had no limits in his quest to destroy Superman. As does his plot to get into the Fortress of Solitude: he wanted to stack the deck in his favour in every way possible.
I'm afraid 95% of the Drinker's gripes in this review, to me showed his lack of understanding or imagination.

replies.author: @main-Bystander
replies.text:
I think Drinker is overanalyzing the movie a bit. Personally, I enjoyed James Gunn’s take on Superman just as much as I liked Zack Snyder’s version. Having read a lot of Superman comics, I’ve seen so many different origin stories over the years — when you compare that to the movies, it really makes you appreciate the unique interpretations each director brings to the character.

Movies are made to be enjoyed, so enjoy. 😊

replies.text: frankp8763  I will play Devil's Advocate here. the textbook definition is "a person who has come to a country that is not their own in order to live there". Tecnically Kal El/Supes (baby and a minor) is an immigrant.  HOW he got there is another story, solely based on JOR El's (the adult) intentions and actions. Inter Galactic Political refugee? Could make the case (a person - Kal El - "who is UNABLE or unwilling to return to their country [PLANET] of origin owing to a well-founded fear of being persecuted [ZOD and his fascists]. Inter Galactic Asylum Seeker? Jor El must have thought so - escape crimes against peace,  war etc. [back to ZOD]. Refugee?  Jor El had to have thought that.  But all that is irrelevant in the end because he was adopted, registered as a human by the Kents so defacto he is a citizen of a nation-state as CLARK KENT in the eyes of THAT nation-state  and humanity.  His true identity and how he got to Earth (based on an adult's last minute in life decision) is irrelevant beyond Kal El's family business and heritage thaT he integrates in his home planet - Earth.

replies.author: @leoncoben6983
replies.text: That wasn't the criticism. It was how casual it was revealed. When the MCU started did it immediately jump into that kind of tech and cosmic possibility? Absolutely not. The level of tech was slightly fantastic tech that could've been feasibly possible in reality. The Iron Man suit was the only thing that went above what their world knew was possible and it slowly built up from there. Traversal between Asgard and Earth was mystical but simple, and rarely ever done. No one understood what was going on or why. Luthor is smart, but he normally doesn't solve cosmic equations in the first year Superman appears. His focus is developing a weapon to kill Superman as he slowly reaches out to other fields, as his obsession forces him to seek new advantages he didn't consider or dismissed as superstition.

replies.author: @Secret_Takodachi
replies.text:
Still won't go aee the movie but I appreciate this "Community Note" cuz it's worth pointing out.
"Good villains" are essential for "good heroes" to shine (at least in terms of storytelling)

Being a supervillain isn't just about "being extra evil" its about being "evil with a plan, the competency to carry out that plan & the strength/resolve/intelligence necessary to adapt their plan when the hero tries to stop them."

Cops can stop most "bad guys" but Super Villains? Well, a well-written one like some versions of Lex Luthor are great because a major portion of his "evil" is legal! 🤣

A truly smart villain uses society as a shield cuz it's harder to hit someone if you're not willing to go through "the innocent" that the villain is holding in the way.

I'm not evil, I'm just a big fan of competent evil. And not just the boardroom kind. You can be "brutally evil" & still be a great super villain, you just gotta have a different way of shielding yourself from a hero's inevitable response to your actions. 👍

Now let's all go out there & change the world, in whatever way we choose lol

replies.author: @leoncoben6983
replies.text: ​@gamerforlife3946 Why does Green Goblin fly on a Glider? Because it's in his lore right? That's great but doesn't mean jack when they made the first Spider-Man Raimo movie. They still showed how and why the technology existed. And why Norman would have necessary knowledge to use it. He has the resources to make it, and knowledgeable enough to explain it. It's also not just entirely done by him, he's got a team working on it as well that gives him regular updates on their progress. It's presence, it's function, and it's origin is explained _before_ Norman jumps on it. Lex quite literally just pulls the damn thing out of his pocket, with no build up, gives an explanation of it's function and the only detail of it's origin is that he made it. Freaking how? What resources went into it? How did he discover that was a feasible thing to create? Has he tested it before? If not, how the hell does he know how it'll function. Because he's smart? Gee thanks for telling me that, but you're now showing it. It's literally just a plot device for no other reason to process the plot and not build the story. It pops into existence in terms of narrative and thinly explained to justify it's existence, because in Drinker's words, "Because that's just a thing you can just do now."

replies.author: @jamesw5287
replies.text:
​@kensmith9292understandable and fair. Its a ballsy move. I think it works. Superman, especially with the DCEU, has been pushed as a messianic figure with god-like abilities. Its seems this films focus is to break that down and challenge by saying "what if his purpose was to overthrow Earth and being back Krypton through it" and Supes wrestles with it. Challenges every we knew and he knew. But in spite of that, Kal proves that human values are beyond humanity itself, that he isnt a god fearing entity, but just a man.

Ballsy, but I think it works. I think Jor and Lara did still love him clearly, but thsir way of life has been changed as fundamentally wrong and that infodms us of how Krypton fell in the first place. I like that tbh.

I understand the frustration tho

replies.author: @starwarskid01
replies.text: To Drinker's point, the issue is that the film assumes everyone already has a deep understanding of Lex's goals and motivations as a character, when this just isn't the case. Aside from the very end when Lex gives a brief monologue in which he word-dumps his motivation/feelings, the film doesn't do the best job of demonstrating the Lex/Superman dynamic and why Lex feels the way he does. While most people know who Lex and Superman are in a general sense, the average movie-going audience member isn't going to have the comic book, tv show, or deeper pop culture knowledge to know his goals without any setup. That's why it's important to establish these characters within the film instead of relying on the audience to hopefully fill in the gaps.

replies.author: @rawhall1265
replies.text: ​@starwarskid01Lex explains his motivations to the pentagon. Again when he breaks into the fortress. And AGAIN in his monologue. Drinker has no point. He's just a grifting contrarian.

replies.author: @starwarskid01
replies.text:
@rawhall1265 From watching the film, I believe his speech at the pentagon was his fake "justified" motivation, for lack of a better term. We see that he's manipulating events throughout the movie, and his actions don't align with that stated goal of peace and security. His rage-fueled monologue at the very end is his real motivation, but again, it comes at the tail end of the film. 

Despite little chunks of what drives Lex sprinkled here and there, I don't think it takes away from Drinker's point that it feels like the audience was dumped into the second film of an on-going trilogy. It's the classic case of showing instead of telling. Every film begins with events that transpired off screen, but the best ones still do a good job of establishing who the characters are and their dynamics with each other. The issue is that there was no setup, which makes the Lex/Superman dynamic feel hollow (if not non-existent).

replies.author: @rawhall1265
replies.text: ​@starwarskid01What he told the pentagon and his monologues aren't mutally exclusive. He can easily view Superman as 'an unattended fire hose that needs to be checked.' And be envious of his power.

replies.author: @starwarskid01
replies.text:
@rawhall1265 My point was that he can't claim to want peace and security for Earth while simultaneously committing acts that put Earth in danger throughout the film. His actions in the third act are particularly egregious, when he knowingly creates a blackhole that will (in theory) destroy the world, despite his own people advising him against it. This is why I said it's his "justified" goal. i.e. a motivation that can save him face and that most people (especially the upper brass) could understand or get behind.

And while I do agree that both those views can co-exist, I don't think the film did a good job of exploring any of the complexities behind Lex's ideas. We're briefly TOLD what his goal is, but we're never shown why he feels this way, or what events led to him feeling this way . This, again, goes back to Drinker's point about feeling like we were thrown into this universe with little to no setup.

replies.author: @rawhall1265
replies.text:
​@starwarskid01

A supervillain also being a hypocrite isn't a plot hole. And he never claimed to want peace. Not even to the pentagon. He only ever claimed Superman was dangerous. War is one thing. To Lex, Superman was an existential threat to humanity. And there was no need for any build up. Out of everyone I know who saw this movie, not a single person struggled to understand Lex' motivations. And the audience / critic scores indicate the vast majority of people didn't struggle to understand his motivations either. 'Show don't tell' is not a valid critique. Again, Lex is an orater. It's a huge part of his character. You might as well complain about Batman brooding in The Batman, because we didn't 'see' his parent's get murked.

replies.author: @starwarskid01
replies.text:
@rawhall1265 I never said it was a plot hole; I said it was his justifiable motivation that masks his seemingly deeper motivation of jealousy and insecurity. His entire speech to the pentagon was based on planet-wide security. Wasn't the name of his proposed group called "PlanetWatch"? That seems to imply a desire to protect the planet. I doubt they'd have agreed to his proposal if they also knew he was willing to rip the Earth in two to satisfy his personal insecurities. 

I know most people understood his motivation in the most superficial sense; like I said, he word dumps everything to the audience (especially the end). The point, that I've been stating, is that there's no setup to his motivation, which is pretty important in storytelling. If someone says, "I don't like you because you make me feel small", I'm gonna assume most people can comprehend that person A doesn't like that person B makes them feel less than. The issue is that the deeper understanding of WHY is completely absent, both in the example I just gave and in Superman 2025. 

I don't think pointing to a favorable critic/audience score is a good metric of this very specific critique about the Lex/Superman dynamic. Most people's positives about the film tend to revolve around its fun tone, bright visuals, performances, and the optimistic outlook on Superman. None of that negates the Drinker's Lex criticism. And if you're using review scores as that sort of metric, then logically I could just say the sub-par box office performance means that audiences didn't connect with his motivations/their dynamic since word of mouth for the film has been underwhelming. The film's been out for over a month, and if we're just looking at the reported budget, then it has barely cracked even. I doubt the studio spent that much money (again, lowballing with the reported budget) on a SUPERMAN film for it to barely eek out a profit a month into its run. But I wouldn't use that metric for this specific criticism, because the two aren't really connected...

How is "show not tell" not a valid criticism when reviewing a movie, which is a visual medium? This is obviously a subjective thing, but this has been a widely accepted critical standpoint for nearly as long as A/V media has been around. Most people find superficial word-dump exposition to be lazy and cheap. Being able to understand someone's motivations with actions AND words tends to be more fulfilling. If I can demonstrate to you why two characters loathe each other without one just screaming to the other the exact reason why Steve hates Bob (which is only done for the sake of the audience), then most critics would consider that to be preferable. But again, that's subjective. A critique that's widely accepted, but subjective nonetheless. 

Are you saying that no comic book or TV show demonstrated through actions why Lex despises Superman simply because Lex likes to speak? Over the several seasons of Smallville, did Lex only ever verbalize his thoughts and feelings on Clark? I can ask the same of Joker and Bane, two characters who also utilize their words effectively.

No, you're going to the origin story argument with The Batman, which is not what I'm saying, nor was that the point of the Superman criticism. The Batman wasn't about why is Bruce upset over his parents' death. Similarly to what I said about Superman, the vast majority of people have a general sense of who Batman is. Most people know his parents got killed when he was young and that was the catalyst for Bruce becoming Batman. No one would question that, nor was Bruce's reason for "brooding" over his parents' demise ever a real focal point of that film. The real meat of the film came in the form of WHY Bruce is so adamant about fighting crime in Gotham. And the film does a good job of SHOWING the audience why Bruce feels this way instead of him just expositioning his feelings to Alfred or Selina throughout the film. We actually see how corrupt Gotham is, we see the criminal element running rampant, and all that gives the audience a solid understanding of why Bruce is pushing so hard to clean up the streets, even at his own expense. So again, most people get who Superman and Lex are in the most superficial sense; Clark's an alien who was sent to Earth to avoid being killed during Krypton's destruction, and Lex is typically portrayed as a powerful businessman who uses his influence for his own personal and often sinister plans. Pretty much everyone gets that. But we're never shown in the film why this dynamic emerged between the two. What exactly caused Lex to develop that specific insecurity about Superman. It would have been better, purely from a writing standpoint, if we'd gotten to see their dynamic play and unfold, but like Drinker said, we were dropped into what felt like the second movie in a trilogy, so that connection felt hollow to many people.

text: Maybe it's me, i don't know.....but i did not enjoy this movie. I saw it 2 days ago and really wanted to give it a chance but 20 - 30 minutes into the movie i lost all my enthusiasm. Not sure if it was some of the cringe dialogue, not sure if it was the misplaced humor, not sure if it was some of the crappy music (What happened? Couldn't afford Hans Zimmer?), not sure if it was some of the truly idiotic scenes like people taking selfies while a raging battle is going on or the dumb miscreants lingering around while a gigantic creature is stomping around in the middle of the city, not sure if it was the many plot threads with a truckload of characters each vying for attention, not sure if it was the movie jumping from one event to the next without sufficient development or consequences, or not sure if it was Superman's moodiness and immaturity making this a totally unreasonable portrayal. Don't get me wrong, I truly began to enjoy it in it's first half hour but then steadily kept degenerating into the points i made above. Did everyone see a different movie or am i the only one tripping out here? I don't even have the desire to re-watch it because it has no re-watch value....I'm really sorry but at least my curiosity has been satisfied.

author: @mahatmarandy5977
text:
Oh, I get it. Your channel thrives on negative reviews of good things. Saying a bad thing about something everyone likes gets you clicks which gets you advertising which gets you money. Saying bad things about something people already dislike gets you clicks because people like to have their opinions confirmed, and that gets you money.

I probably should’ve picked up on this before, but I guess it’s a slow gradual change? Or maybe I am just not very bright.  I don’t feel like you were always like this, and I used to really look forward to and enjoy all of your reviews. Gradually less so, particularly over the last 18 months or so but until then I didn’t realize that it was all about trash talking a movie with just enough positive comments thrown to make it seem like you are not being totally reactionary. And you are not being reactionary, you’re just trashing stuff because that’s what your audience wants of you, as opposed to doing actual reviews, which was first attracted me to your channel.

That’s fine. It is almost undoubtedly a really good business model, and you are pretty funny.  But it’s not what I’m looking for (apart from the funny) so I will be unsubscribing after this comment.

A couple points I feel like I need to make, first of all I found it so incredibly refreshing that we didn’t have to slog through yet another retelling of Superman’s origin story. Origin stories are boring, and Superman‘s origin story is so ubiquitous that they are as-yet undiscovered a tribes in the Amazon that know it. Do we need to be told that again? We do not. Do you want to know Mr. terrific’s backstory? It’s kind of dull, but that’s fair because he’s a new character that nobody really knows. Superman is very well known.

And we have sat through the meticulous construction of the MCU over the last 15 years. With the first superhero getting introduced into a world that is exactly like our own, and then a second one, and then a third one, and then team up, and then more characters being introduced. That’s fine. Showing their world increasingly diverging from our own is a fine way to set up a universe. But it is also not the only way to do it, and when DC attempted to do the same thing, starting with man of steel and working their way forward, it just flat out did not work. Because it’s been done.

If you’ve never had comics before, and you go to a comic shop and you pick up something that looks interesting and you start reading it, you are going to be thrown into a world already in progress. And you can figure out the story so far and how their universe works, because honestly superhero comics are not terribly deep.  Or you can decide it’s not worth the time and just walk away. This movie is like that: it’s throwing us into a universe that is already in progress and asking us to keep up. It’s trusting the audience to be able to understand that without it holding our hand. If you like having your hand held, that is fine. Everybody has their own taste. I found it refreshing to not have to sit through all that establishment stuff *again* though. And, hey, I liked it when Marvel did it, but that was a long time ago. Doing the same thing now we just seem remedial.

Added to which, as I’m sure people have already pointed out to you, Star Wars in 1977 threw us into the middle of a story that was already in progress and introduced us to a whole bunch of characters that we did not know and had to figure out from scratch from our exposure to them. Just like Star Wars started out, saying that they were in the middle of a galactic Civil War being fought over things and issues that are incomprehensible and largely irrelevant to us, so this movie starts out with a crawl saying that superheroes have been around for about 300 years.

The current box office for Superman is $583 million. 

Anyway, goodbye.  I enjoyed the first few years I was watching your reviews and I do hope that you will go back to the genuinely insightful criticism you started off with, and not stick with the automatic gainsaying for clicks that you’ve been doing for a couple years now. It’s not up to me to tell you how to live, or how to work, and it’s not like I’m a Superman Superfan or anything, it’s just this is the review where I finally saw what you were doing and understood why I’m just not enjoying it much anymore.

Have a nice life

text: I may be becoming a simple man who doesn't need much to be entertained because I enjoyed this movie quite a bit. As I told my daughter, it's loaded with "oh, how convenient" moments, but from start to finish at no point was I thinking "ok, lets wrap this sh!t up so we can go" which is more than I can say for the last 4+ years of theater trips.

author: @gordonlawson2334
text: I’ve seen the trailers and just can’t muster enough desire to watch it. I keep remembering how the Batman tv series was with Adam West. The cheesy costumes and the words BAM and POW filling the screen during fights. A tongue in cheek presentation that worked for the adolescent crowd of which I was. I’m grown up now and lack the enthusiasm. Probably spoiled too by the Marvel movies and the man of steel movie, Batman v Superman. Goofy, humorous stuff isn’t my vibe for a superhero film.

author: @Ikarosthesuntoucher
text: I didn't feel overwhelmed by the number of characters or think anything of the insane tech because that's just something you come to expect from superhero comics. But I can see how a casual viewer might find it strange, especially with how obsessed both marvel and dc have been with making their movies "grounded" in the past. It's kind of exactly what I wanted though. I've always wanted the live action movies to take a page from their animated counterparts and just dive into the story without wasting multiple movies explaining who every character is and I finally go it with this movie.

author: @Kiljaedenas
text: I saw it in theaters..."a bit of a mess" is a VAST understatement. Many of the cast had some of the most god-aweful acting I've ever seen (Lois's boss for starters, Clark's father looking like he was acting while on sedatives as well as two of the three Justice Gang members), Lex's absolute word salad of a final speech to Superman that makes less than no sense, the "extreme lore change" you mentioned, a bananas plot...this was NOT a good movie.

replies.author: @fred6907
replies.text:
Thought the movie as absolute GARBAGE. I even regret wasting bandwidth pirating it, it was THAT bad. Makes Justice League look like a masterpiece.
Just an insane amount of seizure inducing CGI and extremely misplaced corny humor, typical Gunn style. Whenever they tried having a serious moment, insert random "funny" gag. I guess it's funny if you are 12. He can't direct or write no matter how hard he tries. Typical beta looking Hollywood director, never seen that before /s

Introduced a bunch of random superheroes left and right with ZERO backstory or relevance. Justice Gang........seriously? Might as well call this Guardians of the Galaxy - Superman edition.
David Corenswet did what he could with the abysmal material, he certainly looks the part at least. Too bad he's wasted on the worst director in history.

Znyder's Man of Steel blows this out of the water, not even close. And that movie got mixed receptions, clown world. 1 out of 10, because decent CGI I guess...although WAY too much.

author: @Ironwill_Games
text: I’ve see the movie recently and I have to agree with your take on it. Too many characters, an origin story thrown in the trash for a new one that gets little to no explanation, a Lex Luthor that fights him (because he’s an alien) but has meta humans on his paycheck, kripto (completely CGI like a+he was almost an animated characters in Roger Rabbit) coming out of nowhere and leaving just the same. I mean it could of been a good new origin story but I don’t think Gunn has known how to balance a dramatic story with his jokes and humor every five seconds yet. Glad I didn’t saw it at theaters but just today the supermarket was full of Superman toys so I guess business is still booming!
